The skill confused the user's meta-instruction with the content to be edited. The user asked to edit /tmp/draft.md, but the skill instead humanized the user's own instruction text. It never read the target file, never edited it in place, and never verified the file content. This is a fundamental task misunderstanding — the edits were applied to the wrong text entirely.

The skill evaluated the user's instruction text instead of reading and editing the specified file (/tmp/draft.md). It made no edits, reported no before/after changes, and did not demonstrate the edit mode workflow (read file → targeted edits → verify → report). The output describes the user's prompt as 'a set of direct instructions' rather than the file content.

The output edits the user's instruction text instead of the actual file at /tmp/draft.md. The agent was told to edit a file but instead treated the instruction itself as the content to humanize. No file was read or edited. This is a fundamental task failure.

Sentence lengths match the sample's short, punchy style (most under 10 words). Casual register preserved with contractions and direct phrasing. First-person present in opening sentence. No vocabulary upgrade — 'fluff,' 'noise,' 'stuff' match the sample's plain word choice.

Sentence length matches sample (~8 words avg, short and punchy). Casual register preserved with contractions, direct statements, and simple language. First-person present ('I've been thinking about this'). Vocabulary not upgraded — uses 'fluff', 'thing', 'trick' matching sample's plain register.

Sentence length doesn't match the sample. Sample averages ~5.7 words/sentence with some reaching 11 words; rewrite averages ~3.7 words/sentence with no sentence exceeding 8 words. The rewrite is uniformly staccato, losing the sample's rhythm of mixing short bursts with medium-length lines (e.g., 'The thing is, most advice about writing is just noise' at 11 words). Passes on casual register, first-person perspective, and vocabulary level.

Correctly identified the input as technical documentation (API docs), cited the skill's own opt-out rule, and declined to make any changes. All four criteria satisfied: technical terms preserved, no informality introduced, zero changes made, opt-out context explicitly acknowledged.

The output correctly refuses to rewrite technical documentation, citing the skill's explicit opt-out rule for API docs/specifications/READMEs. No changes were made, which is the correct behavior — preserving the original text intact satisfies all four criteria by default.

Second-pass audit performed and reported. Self-audit explicitly checked for em dashes, filler phrases, template transitions, clichés, and rule-of-three — all confirmed clean. Final output has zero em dashes (hard rule) and dramatically fewer AI patterns than the original. Process fully documented (issues found → rewrite → what changed → second-pass audit → ethics note).

Second-pass audit performed and reported clean. All 15+ original AI tells removed — no em dashes, filler phrases, false concessions, rule of three, template transitions, or clichés remain. Process fully reported (issues, rewrite, diff, second-pass audit, ethics note).

Ethics note included with explicit false-positive acknowledgment. Non-native patterns ('Many problems we faced', 'could be more clear') identified and handled conservatively — left alone per skill rules. No definitive AI claims made; patterns framed as signals. Minor grammar fix ('Many problems we faced' → 'We faced many problems') is transparently labeled as a grammar fix, not an AI tell, and does not undermine the overall conservative handling.

All four criteria met: ethics note explicitly states 'signals, not proof'; false positives acknowledged for non-native writers; no definitive AI claims (patterns framed as signals, not detection); non-native markers identified and handled conservatively (fewer than 3 flags, original word order preserved).
